enter.speaker: narrator
enter.text: The shop smells of dried herbs, salt, and lamp-oil. Shelves stand behind a woman who does not waste motion. She watches you as you cross the threshold, and by the time you reach the counter she has decided how much of her attention you are worth.

hello.speaker: vira
hello.text: First time in Ashenvale. Good — the regulars are mostly gone, so I won't mistake you for one. What do you need?

choices.text: I heard you need spider silk?
There is a debt note in your ledger you have not collected on.
The matter of the debtor in Duskmere is closed.
The matter of the debtor in Duskmere is closed.
I read the journal. It was for you.
Anything off the counter for someone who has earned it?
Where do you get your stock?

silk-offer.speaker: vira
silk-offer.text: My rope stock is low and the trade road is closed. Spider silk from the depths can be braided into cord that holds better than hemp. I need two bolts. The spinners in Mossbarrow drop it, if you can kill them without shredding the silk.

silk-accept.speaker: vira
silk-accept.text: Bring it clean and dry. I will make it worth your time.

silk-turnin.speaker: vira
silk-turnin.text: Two bolts, clean weave. Good. This will keep the shelves stocked through the month. You are more useful than most of the adventurers Brenna sends through here.

silk-done.speaker: vira
silk-done.text: Forty gold. Come back if you find more — I can always use it.

wares.speaker: vira
wares.text: The usual. Rope, oil, flint, hard tack. A few healing salves — not as good as a cleric's work, but cheaper. Arrows by the dozen. Ask again when I've finished the inventory and I'll have prices.

missing.speaker: vira
missing.text: The trade roads dried up. The Greenhollow route stopped carrying anything worth carrying. Some of the merchants walked back. Some did not. I do not ask after the ones who did not. I ration what I have, and I am careful about who I extend credit to.

rumors.speaker: vira
rumors.text: I hear what the guild hears, a little earlier. An adventurer came through three weeks ago with a piece of old iron he could not explain. He sold it to a woman I did not recognize, for more than it was worth. He tried to buy it back the next morning, sweating, and left without it. I have not seen him since.

rumors-hint.speaker: vira
rumors-hint.text: I don't sell old iron. I sell oil, and salt, and rope. Would you like oil, salt, or rope?

vira-supply.speaker: vira
vira-supply.text: Used to get most of it from the Caldenmere caravan. Salt, oil, dried stock, rope — they came through every fortnight. The last run was eleven weeks ago. Since then I work with what passes through the guild and what I can source locally. Which is not much.

vira-supply-2.speaker: vira
vira-supply-2.text: If you find anything useful out there — pelts, cord, bottles, even scrap tin — bring it here before you sell it to someone else. I pay fair weight.

vira-travelers.speaker: vira
vira-travelers.text: A woman came through last month. Said she had walked from the eastern coast — alone. No pack animal, barely a bedroll. She bought three days' hard rations, a flint, and a good knife.

vira-travelers-2.speaker: vira
vira-travelers-2.text: She asked if the road to Ashenmere was passable. I told her I did not know. She nodded like she had expected that answer, paid, and walked east. I do not know what drives someone to walk into the unknown on an empty stomach. I am glad it is not me.

leave.speaker: vira
leave.text: Come back when you know what you need. I will be here.

vira-rank-offer.speaker: vira
vira-rank-offer.text: Ranked, are you. The seal on your guild token is hard to miss when it catches the lamp. Most who wear that one stop pretending they want rope and oil. So — yes. There is a shelf I do not show to F-rank.

vira-rank-offer-2.speaker: vira
vira-rank-offer-2.text: A vial of tincture from the eastern caravan, six years ago. A whetstone that came off a man who did not finish his last contract. A small mirror that does not show the room behind you. I do not price these. I trade them for stories and for favors. When you have one, bring it.

ledger-offer.speaker: vira
ledger-offer.text: ...You went looking. I should be angry. I am tired instead. The note is for one Halden, fisherman, Duskmere. Forty gold owed against rope and salt and a winter of credit I should not have given. He has not crossed the lake to pay it.

ledger-offer-2.speaker: vira
ledger-offer-2.text: He has a child. The child is sick — has been all year. I know because his wife used to walk over for the moonpetal salve before the road went bad. That is why the note is still in my drawer and not on the slate behind me.

ledger-offer-3.speaker: vira
ledger-offer-3.text: Cross the lake. Find Halden. Close the matter — collect the forty, or pay it yourself, or write it off in some fashion you can live with. I am asking you because I cannot decide, and a shopkeeper who cannot decide loses her shop. Go.

ledger-accept.speaker: vira
ledger-accept.text: Bring me the answer. Whatever shape it takes. Do not bring me sorries. I have a drawer of those already.

ledger-turnin.speaker: narrator
ledger-turnin.text: Vira looks at you for a long moment without speaking. Whatever she sees in your face she puts into the ledger first — a small mark in the margin of the page she has been keeping open for thirty-two days.

choices.text: I collected. Forty gold. Halden paid.
I paid the debt myself. Out of my own purse.

ledger-collected.speaker: vira
ledger-collected.text: Forty gold. Counted clean. Good. The book closes properly, and the door of his cabin still opens, and somewhere a child sleeps a little hungrier tonight. Trade is trade. I will not pretend it is anything else.

ledger-collected-2.speaker: vira
ledger-collected-2.text: Three hundred and twenty gold for the work. Four hundred and sixty experience. The inflated rate is not for the errand — it is for what the errand cost you to run. I have known the cost. Do not refuse the coin.

ledger-forgiven.speaker: vira
ledger-forgiven.text: ...Out of your own purse. Forty gold of yours, on a man you do not know, for a child who will not remember your face. Why did you do that.

ledger-forgiven-2.speaker: narrator
ledger-forgiven-2.text: She does not wait for your answer. She picks up the ledger, draws a clean line through Halden's row, and closes the book. Her hand stays flat on the cover for several breaths.

ledger-forgiven-3.speaker: vira
ledger-forgiven-3.text: One hundred and ninety gold from the drawer. Five hundred and forty experience. The forty you are out, I cannot return — that was your choice, and a shopkeeper does not undo a customer's choice. The rest is what I would have lost twice over if I had sat on that note one more month.

ledger-forgiven-close.speaker: vira
ledger-forgiven-close.text: I will tell you something. I have not paid for a stranger in eleven years. I had begun to forget what kind of person does. Walk in here whenever you like. The shelf I do not show to F-rank — show it to yourself.

vira-journal-deliver.speaker: narrator
vira-journal-deliver.text: You set the journal on the counter. You do not open it. Vira looks at the cover, at the salt-stains, at the small pressed petal slipped between two pages near the back. Her hand stops halfway to it. She does not pick it up.

vira-journal-2.speaker: vira
vira-journal-2.text: ...Edrik. Of course it is Edrik. He bought a flint from me the morning he left, and I told him it was a poor flint, and he laughed and bought it anyway. I had wondered why I remembered a sale that small.

vira-journal-3.speaker: narrator
vira-journal-3.text: Vira opens the back of the book. She reads the last page once. She closes it. She does not read it again. She holds it against her chest for a long count of breaths. When she sets it down, the small mole at the corner of her mouth is the only thing on her face that has not changed.

vira-journal-4.speaker: vira
vira-journal-4.text: He never said the word. Not once across a winter. He paid in moonlight silver, which I should have read as a question, and I priced it instead. That is the kind of woman a shop makes of you. I am sorry, Edrik. I am sorry I read the book later than the writing.

vira-journal-5.speaker: vira
vira-journal-5.text: Two hundred and eighty gold. Four hundred and twenty experience. Tell Tomas his guest is at peace. Tell him from me, please. I cannot say it across the square. The shelves need watching.
